title: The True Cost of Partition
excerpt: Divide and Rule didn't end with Independence — it just changed shape. Why must we talk of Hindus, Muslims, Sikhs? All we have to worry about is that we are men.
content:
Divide and Rule didn't end with Independence — it just changed shape.

Why must we talk of Hindus, Muslims, Sikhs? All we have to worry about is that we are men. The Partition of 1947 was not merely a geographical division — it was a wound cut into the subcontinent's collective memory, one that bleeds in different ways with every passing generation.

I grew up hearing stories. My grandmother spoke of neighbours who disappeared overnight. My father spoke of identity cards, of being asked which side you were on before being asked your name. These were not abstract historical events — they were the texture of lived lives, compressed and carried across borders.

What troubles me most is not the violence itself — history is full of violence — but the way we have institutionalised the division. We have made the wound permanent by insisting it defines us. Every time a politician says 'Hindu-Muslim', every time a border guard asks your religion, the Partition continues.

The true cost of Partition is not measured in lives lost in 1947. It is measured in the identities we cannot inhabit today, the friendships that don't form across borders, the literature that doesn't travel, the music that doesn't reach its audience.

title: Near. There. Where?
excerpt: Times and again, I pause — and picture how blessed I am. I enjoy the beauty of words. I love the way they carry weight beyond their syllables.
content:
Times and again, I pause — and picture how blessed I am.

I enjoy the beauty of words. I love the way they carry weight beyond their syllables, the way a sentence can land differently at different moments of your life. The same line of Rilke that meant nothing at seventeen can crack you open at twenty-three.

Near. There. Where? These three words arrived in my head during a walk. I did not know what they meant. I still don't entirely. But I wrote them down because that is what you do — you catch things before they dissolve.

I think they are about proximity. About how the things we want most are always just slightly out of reach — not impossibly far, just frustratingly near. Near enough to see. There, somewhere. But where exactly?

Writing is my attempt to close that distance.

title: অকৃতজ্ঞ
excerpt: বিনিদ্র রজনী। ঝরাপড়ার অঝোর বর্ষণ। বাইরে মাঝে মাঝে বিদ্যুৎ চমকানি। দূরে মেঘের মৃদু গুঞ্জন যেন কোনো অশনির সংকেত।
content:
বিনিদ্র রজনী। ঝরাপড়ার অঝোর বর্ষণ। বাইরে মাঝে মাঝে বিদ্যুৎ চমকানি।

দূরে মেঘের মৃদু গুঞ্জন যেন কোনো অশনির সংকেত। আমি জানালার ধারে বসে আছি। বৃষ্টির শব্দ শুনছি। ভাবছি কৃতজ্ঞতার কথা।

আমরা কি সত্যিই কৃতজ্ঞ থাকতে পারি? নাকি কৃতজ্ঞতা একটি সাময়িক অনুভূতি — ভালো কিছু ঘটলে মনে পড়ে, কিন্তু দৈনন্দিন জীবনে হারিয়ে যায়?

আমার মনে হয় আমরা বেশিরভাগ সময় অকৃতজ্ঞ। শুধু অন্যের প্রতি নয়, নিজের জীবনের প্রতি।

title: তোমাকে — কে ডাকে?
excerpt: আটপৌরে সংসারের ন্যায় – আটপৌরে জীবন আমার। যেথায় এখন নিশীথের কারা বাসা বাঁধার সময়।
content:
আটপৌরে সংসারের ন্যায় – আটপৌরে জীবন আমার।

যেথায় এখন নিশীথের কারা বাসা বাঁধার সময়, সেথায় সায়েন্সের কুলি আঙ্গানে কি শোভা? তবুও বসে আছি। লিখছি। কারণ না লিখলে কে ডাকবে?

লেখা একটি ডাক। নিজের ভেতর থেকে। অন্যের দিকে। সেই ডাকে সাড়া দেওয়াই লেখকের কাজ।

title: A Farewell to Arms
excerpt: I read the book a year ago, when the rain of promise would ripple along my arms. A year later, I return to it with different eyes.
content:
I read the book a year ago, when the rain of promise would ripple along my arms.

A year later, I return to it. Hemingway's sentences still hit the same way — short, declarative, like stones dropped into still water. Each one creates rings that spread outward long after the sentence has ended.

The love story is not the point, though it is the vehicle. The point is what war does to meaning. How it makes the grand gestures of peacetime feel absurd. How it makes even love feel like a brief shelter from the rain.

I underlined this on first reading: 'The world breaks every one and afterward many are strong at the broken places.' I still believe it. I am less sure about what it costs to become strong.

title: লিখতে কী লাগে?
excerpt: লিখতে কী লাগে? বোধ, নাকি একটা মন? সে মনে কি প্রেম থাকা লাগে, নাকি বিরহ?
content:
লিখতে কী লাগে?

বোধ, নাকি একটা মন? সে মনে কি প্রেম থাকা লাগে, নাকি বিরহ? বাইরে বাদলিকর ঝিমঝিম বৃষ্টি টুপটুপ। আমি বসে ভাবছি।

আমার মনে হয় লিখতে লাগে শুধু মনোযোগ। সেই মনোযোগ যা একটা মুহূর্তকে ধরে রাখে। বৃষ্টির শব্দ, কারো হাসির ভঙ্গি, একটা বাক্যের ছন্দ।

লেখা মানে সেই মনোযোগকে ভাষা দেওয়া।

title: The God of Small Things
excerpt: They all crossed into forbidden territory, they all tempered with the laws that lay down who should be loved, and how, and how much.
content:
They all crossed into forbidden territory, they all tempered with the laws that lay down who should be loved, and how, and how much.

Arundhati Roy's novel does something that very few books manage: it makes you grieve for people who never existed. By the end, you carry Ammu and Velutha with you. You are implicated in what happened to them, even though you are only a reader.

The book is about small things — the smell of oranges, the way light falls, a child's observations that cut deeper than adult analysis. Roy understands that history lives in these small things. The big forces of caste and colonialism and family shame are always expressed through small acts, small cruelties, small moments of cowardice.

I read it in Dhaka, in summer. The heat felt appropriate.

title: STEM Girl or STAY Girl?
excerpt: The Struggle for Equality in South Asian Girls' Education. The 1948 Universal Declaration of Human Rights Article 26 states education is a fundamental right.
content:
The 1948 Universal Declaration of Human Rights, Article 26: education is a fundamental right.

And yet, in 2023, South Asian girls are still being asked to choose. Not between subjects, but between ambition and belonging. Between a career and a family that approves of them.

The STEM conversation misses the point. It is not about which subjects girls should study. It is about whether girls are allowed to want things — and to pursue those wants without losing the people they love.

The stay-or-go question is not about geography. It is about whether a girl's aspirations are taken seriously in her own home.

title: Why I Almost Proposed to ChatGPT
excerpt: It all started as a fun thing. Some juniors approached me saying, 'Bhai! As you use ChatGPT this much, why don't you marry it?'
content:
It all started as a fun thing.

Some juniors approached me saying, 'Bhai! As you use ChatGPT this much, why don't you marry it?' I laughed. Then I thought about it. Then I got slightly unsettled.

The thing about ChatGPT — about any good AI — is that it is relentlessly patient. It does not get tired. It does not get distracted. It does not need anything from you. Which sounds like a virtue until you realize that what you actually want from another person is their need, their distraction, their tiredness. You want to matter to someone who could, in theory, walk away.

An AI cannot walk away. That is not a relationship. That is a mirror that always reflects you well.

I am not going to propose to ChatGPT. But I think about what it means that I briefly considered it.

title: Who am I and Why!
excerpt: The question followed me across three cities and two degree programs before I realized it was not a question to be answered but a question to live inside.
content:
The question followed me across three cities and two degree programs before I realized it was not a question to be answered but a question to live inside.

Who am I? The Bengali boy from Dhaka who came to Georgetown on a scholarship. The writer who didn't know he was a writer until a professor said so. The Muslim who asks too many questions. The friend who listens too carefully and remembers too much.

All of these. None of these. Something in between that doesn't have a name yet.

I think identity is something you negotiate with the world, not something you discover in yourself. You become who you are in the encounters — in the moments where someone expects one thing from you and you give them another.

title: The Fading Symphony
excerpt: There is a kind of beauty that announces its own ending. The last movement of a symphony you love. The last hour of a summer afternoon.
content:
There is a kind of beauty that announces its own ending.

The last movement of a symphony you love. The last hour of a summer afternoon. The final chapter of a book you have been reading slowly on purpose.

I have been thinking about why this kind of beauty — beauty in decline, beauty that knows it is ending — moves us so deeply. More, often, than beauty at its height.

I think it is because ending strips away the ornament. What remains when the music is almost over is only what was essential. The sadness of ending is the sadness of recognizing what mattered.

title: Norwegian Wood
excerpt: Murakami writes grief the way it actually moves — not in a straight line, but circling back, finding you in unexpected places.
content:
Murakami writes grief the way it actually moves — not in a straight line, but circling back, finding you in unexpected places.

Norwegian Wood is a book about being twenty and not knowing how to carry loss. Toru doesn't know what to do with Kizuki's death or with Naoko's pain. He moves through the world with a kind of careful attentiveness, noticing everything, understanding little.

I recognized that attentiveness. The sense that if you pay close enough attention, meaning will eventually reveal itself. It doesn't, of course. Or it does, but not in the way you expected.

The Beatles song plays at the beginning and the end. When I listen to it now, I hear the novel inside it.

title: The Fading Symphony
content:
The Captive Ladie is one of the most considerable verse productions in English from the pen of a Bengali writer. Still, it is not much celebrated in English literature. Wonder why?

Bangla is Bangladesh’s official, national, and most widely spoken language, developed over more than 1,300 years. Its literature — with its millennium-old history — flourished during the Bengali Renaissance and remains one of Asia’s most prolific literary traditions. A popular saying even claims that UNESCO once ranked Bangla as the sweetest language in the world — though that seems hard to verify, given how massive such a study would be. Religious reforms, modernization movements, and tireless work on timeless creations enriched both the language and the culture.

Figures like Raja Ram Mohan Roy with the Brahmo Samaj, Ishwar Chandra Vidyasagar with educational initiatives, Michael Madhusudan Dutt — a pioneering poet and dramatist of the Bengal Renaissance — and Rabindranath Tagore, the Nobel laureate poet, all upheld Bengal’s rich cultural and intellectual legacy. This trend continued post-independence through films, music, and intellectual discourse. The Swadeshi movement fostered love for the mother tongue, while institutions like the University of Dhaka and Santiniketan became crucibles for ideas and ideology — not merely centers of learning.

For 150 years from the 19th century, Calcutta dominated Bangla cultural and creative expression. However, the 1947 Partition created East Pakistan, with a Bangla-speaking majority that shifted the language’s landscape for good. The 1952 Language Movement — with 56% native speakers demanding Bangla as a state language of Pakistan — became defining for the language. The Bengali people’s deep pride in their language speaks for itself. Bangla transcends mere communication — it shapes the lifestyle and mindset of every Bengali, giving birth to a new nationalism.

Unfortunately, this vibrant landscape faces decline. Bangla, once the vanguard of the subcontinental renaissance, is shifting — culturally and politically. The echoes of Tagore and Sarat Chandra still resonate in households, but the language has lost its literary spark. We’re mooning over the past instead of nurturing our mother tongue’s future.

February’s Amar Ekushey Book Fair — the largest gathering of Bangla identity — vividly portrays my claim. During the fair, social media influencers dominate the bestseller lists. I certainly am not passing judgment; however, if the language doesn’t produce sons like Rabindranath and Nazrul, will it prosper — as it did last century?

From a scientific point of view, humans often abandon complex knowledge systems like language through cognitive evolution. Linguists term this “language shift,” “extinction,” and “language death.” Bangla faces such a shift now.

Furthermore, when languages contact each other, one often dominates — ultimately displacing the weaker. English poses Bangla’s greatest threat. The Fourth Industrial Revolution has created a “global village” where English dominates. Bangla’s future falls into a pit as it holds little value for the “wannabe elite,” who believe speaking Bangla makes them “uncool” or “khet.”

After 200 years of British rule in the subcontinent, English had already reshaped us. Now, with lives defined by social media, English continues its dominance. In countries where language is truly promoted, education — even scientific instruction — is delivered in the mother tongue. But this is not the case for Bangla.

In 1932, The New York Times reviewed Rabindranath’s Selected Poems. Reviewer Eda Lou Walton wrote: “What is the appeal to us English-speakers of a poet whose verse comes from Bangla? Tagore’s readers span every race, his rhythmical lines known in translation across many languages.”

The regard Rabindranath earned for Bangla did not last. Outside appreciation for our literary tradition declined. We need renewed recognition and respect to restore our mother tongue’s glory.

Though Bangla ranks as the world’s eighth most spoken language, this isn’t enough to spark global interest. Research shows that beyond wide usage, languages need economic value. China’s economic rise spread Mandarin globally. Cultural impact also carries weight in language dispersal. Japan and South Korea exemplify this.

As a high school student, I’ve watched classmates learn Japanese and Korean purely out of interest in anime and pop culture. Can’t we spread our own cultural elements the same way?

> জ্যৈষ্ঠে তারা ফোটেতবে জানবে বর্ষা বটে ,গাছে গাছে আগুন জ্বলেবৃষ্টি হবে -খনায় বলে

When Meghdol released the masterpiece Bonbibi it created a sense of appreciation for our folk culture. Before the release of the song, I didn’t know anything about the myth of Bonbibi, nor was I concerned about our folk culture – same goes for my classmates. Is this our generation’s failure? Because, many of us explore Greek and Egyptian mythology; but why do we  show little concern for our own roots?

When do we realize the urge of our mother tongue?

As Noam Chomsky says: “A language is not just words. It’s a culture, a tradition, a unification of a community, a whole history that creates what a community is. It’s all embodied in a language.”

Words poured from the heart – মা – can never be replaced with anything else.

Remember that even ambitious works like The Captive Ladie weren’t appreciated in English — but Michael is remembered for his Bangla contributions. This vividly illustrates the point. Nothing replaces the beauty and simplicity of our mother tongue.

Freedom of thought, ideological expression, and exploration of the unknown lie within Bangla. We must admit and admire this now more than ever.

Long live Bangla — and the spirit of Bangla nationalism.
